item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who's reading this in 2018??!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if they stay inside when the doorway closes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I don't care if everyone else think this is too "emotionally charged" or the test logs are too cheesy. It fits.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made an "awww" noise.
I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An okay-to-meh exercise in feels ruined by an expodump at the end that sucks out all narrative ambiguity and doesn't add much that couldn't be better expressed elsewhere. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my experience that indicates a field operative- someone who retrieves objects, secures them, carries out procedure.

From the Security Clearance Levels guide, writing guide tab:

  • Agents are Field Agents, one of two basic types of personnel who primary operate outside of a Foundation facility:
    • The first type of Field Agent is essentially an undercover or embedded agent, working at a police station, hospital, or other anomalous activity hotspot where they can monitor events and notify their superiors of any suspicious activity. These personnel are often unarmed and not equipped to handle any serious anomalous activity; their job is to call for help as soon as something strange starts happening.
    • The second type of Field Agent is the investigator or detective, a person in a suit or other inconspicuous outfit whose job is to snoop around areas where strange things are suspected to be happening and determine whether the situation is one that the Foundation needs to intervene in. While they may carry some basic weapons, they are not soldiers either; their job is also to make a call for backup when such anomalies are confirmed.

You're probably thinking of response team or MTF members?

The kinds of questions asked in the interview log sound like they'd be more likely to come from research staff- a "Researcher" or a "Dr.", someone more concerned with understanding the thing.

There are plenty of interviews carried out by agents. In fact, agents may overall be better qualified to carry out interviews based on the specialized training they receive in regards to interacting with others, whether civilians or anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why would the Foundation make all that information available in the article?

It seems fairly relevant to understanding the anomaly.

- but releasing it to everyone is still dumb, because there could be unnecessary panic over whether these things exist at every museum.)

Where are you getting that they are releasing this information outside of need-to-know distribution? I'm kind of confused by your statement here.

The Foundation representative who spoke with the man at the conference table was referred to as an "Agent". In my experience that indicates a field operative- someone who retrieves objects, secures them, carries out procedure. The kinds of questions asked in the interview log sound like they'd be more likely to come from research staff- a "Researcher" or a "Dr.", someone more concerned with understanding the thing.

I don't think that it really is all that cut-and-dry. In this case, it seems to me, they are treating the interior of the anomaly as a field operation. Which is, IMO, logical as it is a pocket universe.

As written, the Foundation doesn't have a good reason to keep sending visitors to SCP-2018-1. It keeps the museum stable, but the stability of the museum doesn't seem to have any effect on the Foundation's goal of keeping it contained. If they just walled off the entrance in our world, the museum would wear out and fade away…

The Foundation maintains this for the same reason they keep feeding biological anomalies. The Foundation keeps things in containment, to study them, and also because that is what they do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is interesting, and I like the test logs- this is definitely an idea you can understand better with examples.

I like the idea that it makes you feel things for the sake of making you feel things- any emotional response will do, and it's not using them to manipulate you or for any other "sinister" purpose. It takes a prominent and sometimes over-the-top or tacked-on aspect of other SCP concepts, shifts it to an in-universe perspective (instead of the author trying to give the reader feels with the article, the SCP is trying to give the characters feels with its properties), and makes it the center of the concept instead of a peripheral.

I even like the idea of it explaining itself, because (according to its own description) it needs visitors to keep itself stable, and the Foundation wouldn't normally provide them- they only want to keep it contained; they don't care if it wears down, especially since it doesn't even exist in our universe.

I do have a couple of problems with it, though.

+ show block
– hide block
  • The explanation goes into much more detail than is necessary. Even if the guy at the conference table did decide to tell the Agents that the Museum is a genius loci, that it used to be the genius loci of the Australian Museum, and that it just decided to do something different instead… why would the Foundation make all that information available in the article? You could cut all of that out, just leave the parts where he identified as a voicebox for the Museum and told them what it did with visitors, and it wouldn't change the Foundation's perspective at all. The only important part of the origin story is the part that tells them what it wants visitors for. (The part about it having previously been the normal Australian Museum is significant, because it gives the Foundation a way to confirm that it doesn't damage its visitors- they can check on people who've been to or worked at the Australian Museum in the past, and verify that they're not in any worse shape on average than the general population- but releasing it to everyone is still dumb, because there could be unnecessary panic over whether these things exist at every museum.)
  • The Foundation representative who spoke with the man at the conference table was referred to as an "Agent". In my experience that indicates a field operative- someone who retrieves objects, secures them, carries out procedure. The kinds of questions asked in the interview log sound like they'd be more likely to come from research staff- a "Researcher" or a "Dr.", someone more concerned with understanding the thing.
  • This is the big one: As written, the Foundation doesn't have a good reason to keep sending visitors to SCP-2018-1. It keeps the museum stable, but the stability of the museum doesn't seem to have any effect on the Foundation's goal of keeping it contained. If they just walled off the entrance in our world, the museum would wear out and fade away… and as far as I can tell, the Foundation wouldn't even notice. I can see a few easy ways to rectify this one, with varying degrees of creepiness. I think the best fit for the concept would be that denying it visitors affects the anomaly connecting it to the Foundation's universe: if 2018-1 can't charge its batteries from its own visitors, it has to plug into its previous stable power source (the normal Australian Museum) to keep itself running, and the anomaly that connects it to our world expands slightly or moves as a result.

Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IT'S IN MY HEAD!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this one, though I felt that the lockdown was a bit unneeded. Even if his story were false, there were no recorded effects happening outside his little area, so an info lock in excess of the normal security measures a foundation facility might take seems a bit unwarranted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, I was totally going to +1…
…but then Agent was a d!ck…

…it was just Fred the Pug, c'mon guy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: to echo what several others said above, I found this clunky, emotionally manipulative, and none too subtle. Furthermore, the writing seems very sloppy and the sentence structure is awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for museums. I rather like this refreshing bit of fresh air. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other people say it's too explicit, but I think that was a nice change. I guess I'm the only one here who actually sometimes likes things to be explained instead of shadowed in a mystery of vague references, [REDACTED]'s, and endless chains of forum links. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept, but I thought the dialogue at the end was pretty clumsy. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
K. I unvoted.
Apparently that's not a word. But I'll make it one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that you used a genius loci. I will agree that a little less explicitness in the interview would be a good thing, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your idea makes more sense. I'll use it.

Edit: I changed the ending as you suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's actually part of what I didn't like. Why would the foundation even offer him a dog? Would have liked it better if he responded to receiving his dog with "Oh, well there's no way they're going to let me keep this anomalous pug, you may as well take him back."

Just seems like every bit of this SCP is trying to be cute and friendly, even the foundation response.

I can read the thoughts and memories of anyone who enters the museum.

Expected response: So you're reading my mind right now and used what you found to create a zombie dog? Let's get out of here, lock it down, information breach!!
Response I got: Awesome! Got any other cool powers? I love you Fred!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one. No, it doesn't have the surprise punch of some others, you don't really get that penny-drops moment, but it's a solid concept.

And I was already planning to upvote when I hit

When offered a similar dog, Agent ████ refused.

Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea itself isn't bad, but it's too explicit, and pretty ham-fisted in some places. Once I knew the gist nothing really surprised me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of this one, but I feel like I've seen it before, which is not a great thing. Wording is also kind of awkward through out the article. Neutral vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They took the dog back?! Cold hearted bastards….. I also don't mind the emotional part mainly because that's what the very skip is. Just an entity who likes the feels. It's a bit refreshing to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an interesting central idea here, with the personalised museum and everything, but I don't like the test logs - with the exception of the drowning one and the shooting, they all seemed like overly cheesy attempts to wring feels out of the audience. I'm also not an enormous fan of the entity behind this being quite so personable - it's refreshing to have an SCP be forthcoming about their origins, effects, etc. but seeing as it's a genius loci I was expecting a something a little less… human. That's probably more a matter of taste than a real problem with your article, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, it's simplistic in nature enough. I have no problems with it, other than the occasional spelling or grammatical error, and i feel that it is too emotionally orientated. Better if the page was left without test logs, and those were incorporated into a tale or something.
+1 though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a good central idea, but far too explicitly explained…and too obviously emotional.

I'm sure others will disagree, but I think the emotional impact of a skip should come from the story pieced together by the reader from clues in the text. The reader is the one who does all the interpretative work, and the text should provide a bare minimum of clues compatible with allowing them to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP was created in response to Bright's Cliche Challenge. I got number 4 and 5. The song this is based on is "The Kids Aren't Alright" by The Offspring.
